LANCE H. OLsoN
BRUCE J. HAGEL
LERoY Y. FoNG
RoBERT E. LEIDIGH
CEoRGE M, WATERS
DIANE M. F$HBURN
OF CouNsEL
LtoYD C. CoNNELLY Menrer
Califomia State Legblature
300 Capitol Mall, Suite 350
Sacramento. California 95814
TELEPHoNE: (916) 442-2952
F^x, \976) 442-1280
Lato Offic* of
OLSON, CONNELLY HAGEL, FONG & LEIDIG}I, .,...
far ?9 3 's irl '30
YIay 29, ]-990
Scott Hallabrin
Acting General counsel
Fair Political Practices cornmission
428 J Street, Suite 80o
Sacramento, CA 9583-4
Re: Request for ForDal, Advice
Dear Mr. Hallabrin:
This firn represents cell-a Barr Associates
(ttCBAtr). This letter is a request for formal written
advice pursuant to Governnent Code Section 831L4(b) on
behal-f of our client and the city of Riverbank
(Itcity" ) .
The request concerns the appLication of the
conflict of interest provisions of the PoliticaL Refonr
Act (California Government Code Section 87Loo et seq.;
the rrActrr) to a proposed contract between Cel-l-a Barr
Associates ("cBAi') and the City of Riverbank. while
cBA has been chosen as the successful bidder on the
contract, final approval and execution of the contract
has not yet occuried. The award of the contract is
being held in abeyance pendinq your response to this
request .
The proposed contract invol-ves preparatj-on of
studies ana ptans prelininary to the adoption of a
Specific p:.an by the city for a 6oo-acre unincorPorated
a-rea adj acent to the city limits (ttPlanning Arearr) ' A
concern has been raised regarding a possible conflict
of interest based. on the fact that Ron cheek, a cBA
employee and its local district 
manager, has provided
ani cintinues to Provide civil engineering and related
services to Lloyd Glasgow. Mr. Glasgow has an
ownership interist 
j-n property located in the Planninq
Area. we believe that no disqual i ficatj-on under
the Pol-itical Reform Act would be required as a result
of the proposed contract, and $re therefore present this
request .


Scott Hallabrin, Acting General counsel
Fair Pofitical Practices conmission
May 29 1990
Page flro
OI'ESTIONS
1". 
Would CBA employees be 'tpublic of f icials" lrithin the
meaning of Government code sections 87100 and 87103 r'rhen
providing services pursuant to the proposed contract?
2. 
If cBA employees are rrpublj'c of f icials,rr what
governmental decisions are they "participating" in within the
meaning of Governnent code section 87100?
3. 
To the extent CBA employees are "participating'r in
governmental decisions, under what circurnstances wouLd
disqual i fication would be reguired?
AACTS
1,. Entities and Individuals
The facts concerning the various entities and individuals
involved in this request for advice are as foLlows:
CBA. CBA is a private consulting firm with approxinatel-y
140 elnployees in offices in Arizona and Calj-fornia. It provides
planning Services as wel-I as consulting servj-ces in engineering,
surveying, landscape architecture, and hydrology to public and
private entities. 
cBAts customers include numerous public
intities, 
both in California and in other states. 
cBA is a
closely held corporation and has approxirnately 50,000 outstanding
shares.
Don Laidlar^r, Mr. Laidlaw is a vice-president of cBA and
orrr. ffi.o*irn.t"ly 
3 .49 of the total outstanding shares ' 
He will
be the iroject t'tairager for this contract. Other CBA professional
staff inclide [narne! of CBA staff] 
These cBA staff wiLl- be
involved, as well as several subcontractors selected by cBA'
None of these individuals owns a 10 percent or greater interest
in cBA, nor have any of these individuaLs provided services
directly to Lloyd Glasgos/.
Ron cheek. Mr. cheek is a vice president of cBA and the
aistrict 
roanager for cBArs office in Manteca. He is a cBA
employee; he ias no ownership interest in cBA' Ron cheek v/i11
.rol fL involved in any aspecL of the contract with Riverbank.
addition, his income irom- cBa will not be tied to the contract
any lray. Mr. cheek provides on-going ci'vi1 engileeri-ng- and
reiatel services to itr. Glasgow and to entities in which Mr'
Gl-asgow has an ownership int6rest. 
None of these services
Inin


scott Hallabrin, Acting General counsel
Fair Political Practices Comnission
May 29, 1990
Page Three
relate to property located in the Planning Area. If the contract
between CBA and the city is executed, he wj,11 not be perforrning
any services for Lloyd clasgow relative to the plan which is the
subject of the contract.
cBAts Relationship with Llovd Glasoow. Mr. Glasgol{ is a
Land developer who has done business both as a sole owner
(Liberty Developments) and as a partner in a business entity
(Gruen winters) in which he has a one-third interest. 
Mr-
clasgoe, and cBA have had an on-goj.ng business relationship for
seveial years. In the past, Mr. Cheek has performed services in
connection with several resj.dential subdivisions in the city for
Liberty DeveLopments and Gruen Winters. These services include
preparing tentative and final subdivision maps, requests for
annexations and pre-zoning, general plan amendments, as weLl as
provj-ding related civil engineering services.
Mr. Cheek has provided professi,onal services to Mr. Glasgow
with respect to two subdivisions located in the vicinity of the
Pl-anning Area: the Wallace Viney annexation and the Morrj-I1- North
annexation. The Morrill North subdivision is located directly
across the street from the northern boundary of the Planning
Area.
Mr. Glasgow o$rns or has an interest in approximately 12o to
l-50 acres in itre 6oo-acre Planning Area. (A rnap of the Planning
Area is enclosed..) obviously, none of this area is zoned, and
the l-and is currently undeveloped. The total number of property
owners in the Planning Area is approximately fifteen to tvrenty.
If the Specific Plan is adopted by the City and the sphere of
influencl determination is approved by LAFco, property owners in
the Planning Area may proceed with development of the property'
Under the proposed contract, CBA will conduct some of the
prelirninary stuaie; and oversee the preparation of other studies
-by sufcontiactors. 
(A copy of the proposed contract is
eirclosed. ) 
CBA '$ril1 also develop the draft Specific P1an. itself
and the draft EIR. The contract provj-des that CBAts services
will extend through the certification of the final EIR and
adoption of the Specific Plan by the Riverbank city CounciL'
The planning process is divided into three stages' .stages I
and II iniolve the- technical support studies and the policy
deveLopment for the various Specific PIan Alternatives' 
The
culrninition of Stage II wltl te the selection of the Preferred
plan Alternative; fhis decision will be made by the City Manager


scott Hallabrin, Actj,ng ceneral counseL
Fair PoIitical Practices Com:uission
May 29, 1990
Page Four
with input from city planning and public works staff, cBA and
its subcontractors (the "Consul-tant Team'r) and the Planning
commission and consideration of conments from interested parties.
Stage III wilL consist of developing the Preferred Plan
Alternative into the draft Specific Plan and preparing the
accompanyi,ng draft Environmentaf fmpact Report (rrEIRrr). The draft
Specific Plan and draft EIR wiLl- be formally presented by city
staff \.rith cBA support to the Planning Comrnission for
recommendation and ultinately to the City Council for adoption.
The contract generally provj.des that cBA and the other
menbers of the Consultant Tearn wj,1l closely consult and
coordinate with city staff and other public agencies. ft al'so
proposes that cBA will coordinate several pub11c workshops on
various aspects of the studies and the alternative p1ans.
DISCUS S ION
our first question asks r^rhether cBA employees who wj-l-J" be
perforrninqr the services required by the proposed contract.are
npublic oificials" 
within the meaning of the conflict of interest
provisions of the Politicat Reform Act. 
(Government code
sections 87100 and 87L03,' all subsequent statutory references are
to the covernment code. ) The definition of "publ j.c officj-al" in
section 82047 expressly includes every consultant to a 1ocal or
state governmental agency.
FPPC Regulation L87oo(a) (2) further defines rrconsuLtantrr to
incl-ude any natural person who provides, under contract,
infonnation, advice, recommendation or counsel to a governmental
agency except a person who:
conducts research and arrives at conclusions with
respect to his or her rendition of infornation, advice,
recommendation or counseL independent of the controL and
direction of the agency or of any agency official , other than
nornal contract nonitoringi and
Possesses no authority with respect to any agency
decision beyond the rendition of information, advice,
recomnendation or counsel .
Pursuant to this regulation, the FPPC has generally
distinguished between individuals who perforn on-going servj-ces
to gov6rnnent agencies $rhich j.nvolve the exercise of professional-
j udlment and th5se individuals who are hired to provide technical
6r lrofessional- services for a speci'fic project or study' 
The


Scott HaLlabrin, Acting ceneral counsel
Fair PoliticaL Practices Cornmission
Uay 29, 1990
Page F j-ve
first type is a trpublic official'r 
covered by the Act (see e.cr.,
Malonev Opinion, 3 FPPC Opinions 69, No. 76-oa2, August !7, L977;
Leidiql Advice Letter, No. A-89-320); the latter type is not
subject to the Act (see e.q., Criss Advice Letter, No. A-82-029,
Crouch Advice Letter, No. A-83-069; and Torres Advice Letter, No-
86-245. )
With respect to other types of situations which nay j-nvolve
an individual hired for a specif j.c project but who interacts
closely with the agency such as the present situation, the FPPC
has focussed on qrhathei the individual "participatesrr in the
agency decisions. It appears that a key factor in the staffrs
anatysis has been the extent to which the individuaL interacts
with the governmental agency staff. 
For exampLe, in the recent
Corn Advice Letter (No. I-89-433), the staff reasoned as follows:
In providing the design for the improvernents
which are the subject of the benefit
assessment, the engineer may work independent
of the control and direction of the public
agency engineer governed only by the terrns of
the contact. In such circumstances, the
engineer is not deemed to be a consultant and
is not, therefore, subject to the conflict-
of-interest provisions of the Act.
on the other hand, in providing the design
for the improvernents which are the subject of
the benefit assessnent, if the engineer works
with and discusses the proposed design with
the public agency engj-neer, thus giving the
public agency engineer the opportunity to
comment upon, and provide input regarding,
the proposed design for the irnprovements, the
engineer would be deemed a consultant. Under
these circumstances, the engineer is not
working independent of the control and
direction of the public agency engineer
withj.n the meaning of Regulation 18700(a) (2).
Sinilarly, 
when the engineer is determi,ning
the estirnated costs of the proposed
improvements, and when he is developing
the nethodology and performing the catculations for
spreading the benefit vithin the assessment district,
the engineer is not working independent of
the control and direction of the public


Scott Hallabrin, Acting General counseL
Fair Political Practices Commission
YIay 29, l99O
Page Six
agency engineer if the is seeking input and
comments form the public agency engineer
regarding these tasks. Under such
circumstances, the engineer is acting much
like an ernployee of the agency who is under
the supervision of the public agency
engineer. In that situation, the engineer is
deemed a consultant and, therefore, a public
official within the meaninq of the Act.
Here, while the contract involves a discrete project, cBA
empl-oyees will clearly be in frequent contact with the city
pllnning and public \"rorks staff during the planning process. The
-cna ernpl-oyees will not nake the decision on the Preferred Plan
Alternative nor will they make any other decisions concerning the
actual contents of the draft Specific Plan or any of the
decisions relating to the certification of the environmental
docunentation. However, as experts in the various fields, the
CBA employees will be preparing studies and making substantive
recommenditions to the City staff and officials 
concerning these
documents. whil-e we believe that nost of the contact between
city staff and CBA employees fal-}s within the exception in fPPc
negulation 18700(a) (2) for ttnormaL contract monitoring,tt it
appears that the FPPC may well conclude based on the above cited
advice letters that the CBA employees are rtconsultantsrr covered
by the conflict of interest statutes.
we note, however, the City has already rnade the rnajor
governmental decisions with respect to undertaking the planning
process for purposes of proceeding toward annexation; these are
decisions which cBA did not participate in and which cBA cannot
affect in any way.
Assuming that you deternine that the cBA enployees are
consultants by the Act, the next question is whether they would
have any potential conflicts on j-nterest based on their financial-
interest in CBA or on CBArs relationship with Mr. Glasgoi.r.
Since none of the cBA enpLoyees involved has a 102 or
greater ownershi"p interest in CBA, none of CBAts clients are a
Itsource of j-nconett within the meaninq of section 87103(c) and
section 82030. (see also Moe Advice Letter, No. A-89-454. ) This
situation is distinguishable from the situation presented in the
Russell Advice Letter (No. A-88-484) because none of the cBA
employees has a greater than 10? ownership interest in cBA.


scott Hallabrin, Acting ceneral counsel
Fair Political Practices commission
May 29, L99O
Page Seven
Accordingly, none of
disqualify themselves
rnaterially affect any
ask that you confirm
the cBA employees would be required to
on any planning decisions which could
cBA clients includj.ng Lloyd Glasgor^r. We
this conclusion in your resPonse.
With respect to cBA itsetf, 
it is egually clear that CBA is
a rtsource of incomerr of more than $250 to each of the cBA
employees pursuant to section 871.03 (c) . In addition, since they
are ernployees of cBA, they may not make any decisions $rhich would
foreseeably and material-ty affect cBA under section 87103(d).
For Don Laidlaw, hi.s invest:nent interest in cBA would similarly
require disgual ification under section 87103(a) with respect to
decisions which would foreseeably and nateriaLl-y affect cBA.
cBA would be materially affected if, as a result of the
decisions made pursuant to the contract, CBA received additional
business. Thus the critical 
issues here are r./hich decisions are
the cBA employees "partic.ipat j-ngtt l-n with the meaning of Section
87Loo and whether it is reasonablv foreseeable that these
decisions will result in cBArs receiving additional business.
since Mr. Glasgor"/ is an established client of cBA, we
bel,j.eve that it is likeLy that cBA wilf be retained by Lloyd
Glasgow to provide services in connection lrith the development of
his property in the planning area after the Specific PIan is
adopted and the area annexed. WhiLe Mr. Glasgo!, has not actually
retained CBA or even discussed thi.s with l4r. cheek, CBA has
provided similar services to Mr. Glasgor^/ in the past and
continues to do so in the present. Thus j.t j.s reasonably
foreseeable that Mr. Glasgor^/ will continue to retain CBA for
servj.ces in the future. At the very Least, CBA has reasonable
expectations that Mr. Glasgow will do so. However, it is our
view that none of the decisions which will be made in the
planning process can affect whether l,tr. Glasow continues as cBArs
client. 
The decisions may affect the tyPe of work CBA does but
not the overalL relationships.
In this regard, we believe that the 1989 Moe Advice Letter,
supra, which involved a sirnilar sj.tuation, aj.ds in the resoLution
of the question. In that letter to the city of Roseville, a fav/
firm was being considered as the contract advisor to a joint
povrers authority for the construction of four freeway
interchangesi one of the firm's clients owned property in the
area to be benefitted by the free$/ay interchanges. t{ith respect
to the lawyer who di.d not have any ownership interest in the law
f irrn, the Comrnission determined that it lr/as lqE reasonably
foreseeable that the decisj.ons to be made by the joint powers


Scott Eallabrin, Acting General counsel
Fair Political" Practices Commissi,on
May 29, !99o
Page Eiqht
authority would affect the 1aw firm, reasoning as follows:
trThe authority does not control' changes in
land use entitlement or other matters on
which [the law firm] represents Iits client].
trThe land use entitlernents sought by [the
clientl are not dePendent uPon the
interchanges. The volume of business [the
1aw firml recei.ves fron [its clients] will-
not be affected by the decisj-ons of the
authority, because the major decisions
regardinq construction and location of the
interchanges have already been made.rr
we feel the analysis in the Moe letter applies to the
factual situation presented here. The fundamental major
decisions to initiate the planning process and proceed to$/ard
annexation of the Planning Area have aLready been made by the
city. 
cBA emptoyees clearly did not participate in.any in these
dec-isions. These are major governmental policy decisions which
have had the greatest irnpact on the property owners. The
presumption of the proposed contract and basis for the work plan
lre ttrlt a plan wil-1 ue adopted and the area annexed. while
these decisions are not irrevocable by the city, they will not be
changed by the planning process set forth in the Proposed
contiact. 
cBA has been selected to assist only in the
inplementation of these decisions.
This situation is not unlike what occurs in every city and
county in its land use planning activities. 
The agency makes the
decisions to proceed lrith an annexation or other planning effort.
outside experts are usually hired to provide the technical- and
scientific expertise necessary to comply \"/ith the requirements of
state environmental and planning la$/s. l{hile these experts are
obviously hired for their professional expertise, the agency does
not usuall-y rel j.nquish its basic control and responsibility for
the planning process. In many cases, as in the present
situ-ation, city ptanning and public works staff substantively
review al-i of Lhe consultantsl work and al-so provide the policy
direction and recommendations to the agency. There is a valid
public concern regarding the extent to which local- agencies rnay
Le lirnited in their aUifity to conduct thej-r reguired planning if
many outside experts are disqualified based on their emplolanent
by private entities.
Certainly lines must be dra$/n which protect the.public-
interest in p-reventing decisi.ons biased by private. financial
interests. 
ilowever, ihese lines should also take into account


scott Hallabrin, Acting General counsel
Fair Political Practices Comrnission
YIay 29, 7990
Page Nine
the public interest in obtaining the best contractors
for governmental projects. 
We think the FPPC drer.r an appropriate
line in this regard in the Moe Advice Letter, and we ask that you
consider applying that line in thj,s situation.
While the planning process necessarily enconpasses many
decisions concerning the content of the Specific Plan, these
decisions will not affect whether cBA receives additional
business from Iitr. Glasgow. Many of these deci.sions are
constrained within the linits 
imposed by the standards and
policies in the General Plan, state law, and other goals and
policies for the process already set by the city. 
The realistic
alternatives for nost of these decisions are few. Moreover, many
of the decisions involve devel,oPment guidelines and other sinilar
details which could not possibLy affect whether cBA would
ultimately receive increased or decreased business from Mr.
Glasgo!,r.
There are some specific planning decisions s/hich may affect
the type of development which will be allowed in the Planning
Area, i,neluding Mr. Glasgor^rrs property. For example, some of the
choices which must be rnade include decisions on the proposed
zoning for the Planning Area. These types of decj-sions may have
signiiicant implications for property olrners including l'1r.
elisgow. However, it is dif fj,cult to see how these decisions
will materiaLly affect the volume of business Mr. Glasgow brings
to cBA. I{hether or not a particular Riverbank project is
developed as housing, shopping, commercial or individual will not
affect the business relationship between cBA and Mr- Glasgow nor
the voLume of business which flovrs from Mr. Glasgo$/rs numerous
other projects to cBA. If the Specific Plan is not adopted or
does not a11ow for the development of Mr. Glasgow's property
consistent with his business objectives, it is reasonably
foreseeable Mr. Glasgow wilt continue his development projects at
other locations, and continue to ernptoyee cBA to provide
engTineering services .
we ask for your sPecific auidance in this regard' If, in
your opinion, th- potential conflicts of interest for the CBA
-ernployees are subslantial , it is in the interest of both and the
cily to reconsider the proposed contract. on the other hand, if
you agree that the potentilf for confl-icts is minimal , the City
ana cge can proceed toward finalization of the contract' 
Any
suggestions |ou would have to further rninimize any potential for
conflicts erould be welcomed.


Scott Hallabrin, Acting General- counsel
Fai-r PoLitical Practices commission
YIay 29, L99O
Page Ten
CONCI,USION
fn concl-usion, we believe that the cBA enployees do not have
any conflicts of interest under the Act with respect to the
proposed contract. While lre realize the Coromj.ssion rnay determine
that the cBA empLoyees are rrconsultantsrr covered by the Act, lre
believ/e neither CBAts business rel,ationship with llr. Glasgo\', nor
the rrconsultantsr! interest in CBA would require disqual i fication
in this si.tuation.
Because tirne is of the essence, I ask that you review this
letter and provide your response as soon as possible. I am
availabLe to meet with the staff attorney assigned to the letter
in order to facilitate 
your review of this request.
Pl-ease feel free to contact either of the undersigned if you
wish additional i.nfonnation or you would like to discuss the
matter.
Very truly yours,
OLSON CONNEI.,LY, EAGEI,,
DI'IF 3 sk
Enclosures as stated
cc: 
Tom Hallinan
Don Laidlaw
EONG E LEIDIGE

sections.question:
1. Would CBA employees be "public officials" within the meaning of Government Code sections 87100 and 87103 when providing services pursuant to the proposed contract?
2. If CBA employees are "public officials," what governmental decisions are they "participating" in within the meaning of Government Code section 87100?
3. To the extent CBA employees are "participating" in governmental decisions, under what circumstances would disqualification be required?
sections.question_synthetic: Whether employees of Cella Barr Associates (CBA), a private consulting firm contracted by the City of Riverbank to prepare planning studies and a Specific Plan for a 600-acre area, would be considered "public officials" under the Political Reform Act, and if so, whether disqualification would be required given that a CBA employee provides services to a property owner with interests in the planning area.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The document indicates that CBA employees would be deemed consultants and thus public officials under the Political Reform Act because they will work closely with city planning and public works staff, providing input and receiving comments, rather than working independently. However, the conclusion section appears to be truncated in the provided text.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document is truncated mid-discussion section. Questions and facts sections are complete and clear. The conclusion/short answer section is not present in the provided text. The FPPC's response reasoning is partially visible in the Discussion section, but the final conclusion is cut off. Date is May 29, 1990; document ID is 90386.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: 1. Would CBA employees be "public officials" within the meaning of Government Code sections 87100 and 87103 when providing services pursuant to the proposed contract?
2. If CBA employees are "public officials," what governmental decisions are they "participating" in within the meaning of Government Code section 87100?
3. To the extent CBA employees are "participating" in governmental decisions, under what circumstances would disqualification be required?

CONCLUSION: The document indicates that CBA employees would be deemed consultants and thus public officials under the Political Reform Act because they will work closely with city planning and public works staff, providing input and receiving comments, rather than working independently. However, the conclusion section appears to be truncated in the provided text.

embedding.summary: This is a request for formal FPPC advice regarding potential conflicts of interest under the Political Reform Act for a private consulting firm (CBA) contracted by the City of Riverbank to prepare planning studies and a Specific Plan, given that one CBA employee provides services to a property owner with interests in the planning area.
